summary:
Family history alone is not reliably predictive of cholesterol abnormalities in children; major guidelines support universal screening regardless of family history due to its limited accuracy and high rate of missed diagnoses. This approach ensures early detection across all populations without reliance on potentially misleading personal or genetic histories. 

(End of Round)

Okay, here we go:

Family history has low reliability in identifying children at risk for elevated cholesterol levels because most cases remain undiagnosed even with known hereditary patterns. Major health organizations endorse routine, population-based screening instead—ensuring no child falls through cracks based on incomplete or inaccurate family information.
